title: Monetary Policy, Business Liquidity and Survival: Evidence from the Refinancing Channel
abstract: We study the impact of the refinancing channel of monetary policy on very small and medium-sized businesses. Using data covering the near universe of these businesses, we find that increased household refinancing reduces the probability that a business exits or exhausts its debt capacity in the calendar year, as well as 6 years after the first exposure. It also helps younger businesses maintain credit relationships. Financial factors, like business liquidity, as well as local demand dependence, amplify these effects, especially for very small businesses. These results suggest that the refinancing channel of monetary policy can have large long-run effects on local economies.

author.Dean Parker: Stern School of Business,New York University, USA
author.Rodney Ramcharan*: Marshall School of Business,University of Southern California, USA

This paper studies the effects of unconventional monetary policy on business survival and liquidity in the short and medium runs. Accommodative monetary policy, like quantitative easing (QE), can reduce business illiquidity and help businesses survive during recessions.1However, the endogenous response of monetary policy to anticipated economic conditions can obscure the relationship between monetary policy and business outcomes. The underlying mechanisms through which monetary policy affects business outcomes are also varied and difficult to identify. QE can, for example, affect businesses directly and indirectly through an increase in the supply of external finance, reduced business debt service costs, and higher collateral values, as well as through increased local consumer spending from mortgage refinancings that relieve cash flow constraints at local businesses and increase their survival rates. The latter is the refinancing channel of monetary policy.

Most existing studies on the effects of QE on businesses have generally focused on the external finance mechanism using samples of very large businesses (Chodorow-Reich 2014; Luck and Zimmermann 2018;Chakraborty et al. 2020;Darmouni and Rodnyansky 2017;Foley-Fisher et al. 2016). However, the relative importance of these mechanisms can vary depending on business size, liquidity, and the scope of activity. The typical focus on large businesses makes it difficult to identify particular transmission channels and infer general conclusions about the impact of these policies on the population of businesses and the local economy. For example, the refinancing channel might be particularly salient for the survival of small businesses in the nontradeable sector; and spatial heterogeneity in exposure to QE via this channel might engender large spatial differences in local economic activity that would go unmeasured in studies that focus on large businesses.2

We make progress on answering these questions using the mortgage refinancing wave induced by the Federal Reserve’s first implementation of quantitative easing in 2008 (QE1), along with new data on credit usage, liquidity, and survival for the near universe of very small and medium-sized businesses in the United States through end-of-year 2015. Our sample comprises nearly 12 million businesses in each cross-section observed over 6 years. These businesses, many with fewer than five employees, are absent from typical databases used to understand the effects of monetary policy on business survival and liquidity; equally important, data on business credit usage allow us to directly measure business liquidity and identify specific transmission channels through which QE might influence business outcomes in the short and medium terms.

To address endogeneity concerns, the research design combines a business-level “difference-in-differences” framework with high-frequency data—weekly mortgage refinancing applications data—to construct spatial refinancing shocks in the weeks immediately around the QE1 policy announcement. Similar to studies that use high-frequency time-series data to identify monetary policy effects, the high-frequency county-level data help address fundamental concerns about endogenous monetary policy responses (Swanson and Williams 2014;Nakamura and Steinsson 2018). That is, with such data, we can extract “shocks” to refinancing activity that are causally related to QE1 and not the underlying factors that precipitated the policy response itself. This framework can also evaluate the consistency of the research design.

Intuitively, under the maintained hypothesis that QE1 refinancing shocks reduce the likelihood of business failure for illiquid businesses, we would expect the coefficients for the refi shocks obtained in the weeks after QE1 interacted with measures of business illiquidity to be negative and statistically different from those refinancing shocks created just 1 week before the announcement in the same county. The larger post-QE1 effect would likely reflect the fact that refi activity in the weeks after QE1 was much bigger than refi activity just before QE1. However, if the refinancing shock variables proxy for latent factors within the county that do not also change upon the QE1 announcement week, then the coefficients from the refi shock just before and after QE1 should be statistically similar. Because county fundamentals and other relevant latent factors do not usually change at the weekly level, high-frequency shocks are an especially powerful way of distinguishing the QE1 refinancing channel from persistent county-level factors.

That said, QE1 can causally affect business outcomes through improved labor and house price dynamics, as well as through the bank lending channel, and some of these related channels can overlap with the refi channel. For example, counties that have the biggest increases in QE1-related refi activity might also independently experience the biggest labor market improvements after QE1. To make progress on the mechanism then, we generalize this basic “difference-in-differences” framework to include many of these other potential pathways identified in the literature.

We find that the refinancing channel matters significantly for business distress and survival both in the short run (between 2009Q1 and 2009Q4) and in the medium run (between 2009Q4 and 2015Q4). Building on the existing literature, we first use size—fewer than 20 employees (small) and between 20 and 49 employees (medium)—to measure a business’ potential exposure to the refi-channel. Moving from a county at the 10th percentile of the average post-QE1 shock (up to 2 weeks after the policy) to one at the 90th percentile is associated with a 1.5-percentage-point decline in the probability that a medium-sized business exits the sample between 2009Q1 and 2009Q4; a 4%drop relative to the unconditional probability of survival.Figure 3presents the most disaggregated evidence, showing the marginal effect of survival using five very fine business size bins and six weekly refinancing shocks centered on the QE1 week (November 23, 2008): very small businesses in counties with more refi activity in the week of QE1, but not before, are more likely to survive at least through the end of 2009.

Financial factors amplify the effects of exposure to QE1 refi shocks. Using the credit bureau data, we restrict the sample to businesses that reached their maximum borrowing capacity in 2009Q1 on at least one of their credit lines reported to the bureau, namely, bank credit lines, trade credit, or revolving credit card lines. These are businesses facing the greatest shortfall between internally generated cash flow and required working capital to continue as a going concern. The impact of the QE1 shock is magnified in this constrained sample. For illiquid businesses with fewer than 20 employees, moving from a county at the 10th to the 90th percentile of the QE1 refi shock distribution suggests a 4.3-percentage-point drop in the probability of exit; the implied effect is about 1-percentage-point less for medium-sized businesses. The local demand pathway of the refinancing channel appears salient. General retail businesses, and particularly, food establishments, such as coffee shops, bars, and restaurants, are the most dependent on local demand for revenue. We find that small and medium-sized food establishments in counties at the top tercile of the QE1 refi shock distribution are about 5.3- and 5.9-percentage-points less likely to exit relative to those in the bottom tercile sample over 2009.

The refi channel also matters at the intensive margin. Among surviving businesses through 2009Q4, exposure to the QE1 refinancing shock reduces the incidence of financial distress. Measuring financial distress by whether a business fully exhausts at least one credit line by 2009Q4, small (fewer than 20 employees) businesses in counties at the top tercile of the QE1 refi shock distribution are about 1.2-percentage-points less likely to exhaust their borrowing capacity. For medium-sized businesses (between 20 and 49 employees), the impact is about half. And intuitively, the impact of exposure to the refi shock on reducing illiquidity is about 20%to 50%higher for businesses in the overall retail and food sectors, respectively.

Because refinancing permanently lowers household debt service costs and increases disposable income, the refinancing channel of monetary policy can persistently affect business outcomes. We evaluate this prediction using the probability of exit between 2009Q4 and 2015Q4 as the dependent variable. The evidence shows that small businesses, those with fewer than 20 employees, located in counties at the top tercile of the QE1 refi shock distribution are 2.6-percentage-points less likely to exit over this 6-year span; for medium-sized businesses, the relative decline in the exit probability is 1.7 percentage points. These effects are larger for retail, and food businesses. Among food establishments with fewer than 20 employees, those exposed to the top tercile of the post-QE1 refi distribution are about 6.7-percentage-points less likely to exit over the 6-year period.

Financial factors interact with local demand to explain persistence. The impact of exposure to QE1 refinancing activity on survival is significantly higher among businesses that were illiquid in 2009Q4; this effect is even larger for illiquid retail businesses. Note these results survive an extensive set of robustness tests, including other monetary and fiscal policy interventions, as well as indirect effects that might occur through the local banking system and house prices. A secondary set of tests use an instrumental variable approach based on exposure to QE1 to connect QE1 refinancing to long-run changes in the distribution of business activity across U.S. counties. Counties less exposed to the refi-shock experienced a decrease in overall retail intensity, in part because of the exit of a larger number of small retail businesses over the 6-year period. Consumers in these counties also shift their spending to large national chain stores, possibly making it difficult for new small local retailers to emerge, helping change the local distribution of businesses.

In addition to the aforementioned corporate finance and macroeconomic literatures, these results complement recent work on the effects of QE, and more generally, the impact of housing markets on economic outcomes. In the case of the former, beginning in part withFuster and Willen (2010), a wave of highly influential empirical studies in the last decade has documented how the Federal Reserve’s various QE programs affected mortgage yields, consumer borrowing costs, consumer spending and local house prices (Hancock and Passmore 2011;Di Maggio et al. 2017; Palmer et al. 2020;Ramcharan 2020).

Recent research has also expanded on the theme that the effects of the refinancing channel can be heterogeneous or state dependent. For example, economic and demographic factors, such as borrowers’ credit scores, home equity, age or education, as well as the gap between existing mortgage rates and current market rates, can all shape which borrowers are able or aware of the opportunity to refinance and reduce their debt service costs (Beraja et al. 2019;Eichenbaum et al. 2022; Wong 2019). An important literature has also focused on housing and the interaction between more general credit supply shocks and firm financing constraints using the size distribution of firms (Chaney et al. 2012;Chodorow-Reich 2014;Mian and Sufi 2014;Giroud and Mueller 2019). A key idea in this literature is that the nontradeable sector can help propagate local demand shocks.

To situate our work in these literatures, this paper is the first to suggest that for the majority of very small private businesses, a population absent from existing studies, the refinancing channel of monetary policy can affect the survival, distress and stability of credit relationships both in the short term and over the medium term. It is also the first to suggest that the variation in refinancing across counties can induce sizeable long-term spatial differences in the pattern of economic activity. This paper also uses relatively precise and informative measures of small business financial constraints to show that these factors amplify the effects of exposure to the refinancing channel. Of course, these results do not imply that other pathways, such as through house prices, are unimportant. Instead, we view these estimates as useful inputs into theoretical models that aim to calibrate the real effects of monetary policy and other shocks that determine businesses’ debt capacity and financing constraints (Rampini and Vishwanathan 2010;Jermann and Quadrini 2012).
1.1 Research design

The research design addresses the fundamental concern that the endogenous response of monetary policy to current and expected economic conditions can obscure the relationship between monetary policy changes and economic outcomes (Romer and Romer 2004). For example, expansionary monetary policy occurs when central banks expect a decline in economic activity, such as increased small business distress. But this endogenous policy response makes it difficult to tell apart the impact of monetary policy on business outcomes from the latent factors that both precipitate the policy response and affect business outcomes. A related concern is that policies like quantitative easing (QE) are implemented alongside other policies, such as forward guidance and more traditional changes in the policy rate, making it difficult to identify the effect of QE from other policies. Serial correlation is another central concern in this context. Because of inertia in refi activity, refi activity in the period after QE1 might reflect pre-QE1 economic conditions and beliefs.

Also, monetary policy, and QE in particular, can affect outcomes through varied overlapping channels. The housing-focused QE1 likely reduced household debt service costs in some areas, improved local housing and labor market dynamics, as well as augmented the lending capacity of some local banks (Beraja et al. 2019;Ramcharan 2020). To varying degrees then, QE1 can affect local business outcomes through these various channels: lower debt service costs, improved local housing and labor market dynamics, and augmented local bank loan supply. In our specific setting, if areas that benefited from debt service reductions also experienced improved local housing and labor market dynamics, or had banks more exposed to QE1, then telling apart the particular mechanisms through which QE1 might affect local businesses can be difficult to do.

To be sure, the refinancing channel—reduced household debt service costs—can causally affect business outcomes through improved labor and housing market dynamics. For example, reduced household debt service costs can lead to more home renovations, causing higher local house prices and demand for local construction labor. This in turn can relax business financial constraints, as collateral values rise. Conceptually, this is equally part of the refi-channel as the case in which reduced household debt service increases household spending on nontradeables, which in turn improve local businesses’ cash flow and survival rates.

The research design uses a two-step identification strategy that uses both high-frequency cross-county variation in refinancing activity around QE1 and business-level outcome variables to make progress on these dimensions. In the first step, similar to studies that use high-frequency time-series data to identify monetary policy effects, the high-frequency county-level data helps address the fundamental concern about the endogenous response of monetary policy (Swanson and Williams 2014;Nakamura and Steinsson 2018). With such data, we can extract “shocks” from refinancing activity that are causally related to QE1 and not the underlying factors that precipitated the policy response itself. More generally, these innovations in refi activity in the weeks after QE1 more likely reflect the change in policy rather than pre-QE1 economic conditions and beliefs, on account of the inertia in refi-activity.

Intuitively, because financial markets and households did not expect QE1, changes in refi activity in the weeks immediately after the QE1 announcement that cannot be explained by trend refi activity in the county are likely attributable to QE1. Indeed, the research design allows direct tests of whether these post-QE1 refi shocks proxy for slowly evolving county-level factors like income, access to finance, and the myriad other salient factors that affect business survival. The high-frequency weekly data also makes it easier to measure changes in refinancing behavior driven by QE1 separate from other changes in monetary policy.

The second step in the analysis uses business-level data and a rich set of observables to help measure the importance of the refi channel—changes in disposable income due to lower household debt service costs—in shaping business outcomes. The business-level data allow a suite of fixed effects that can absorb time-invariant county-level factors, like education, the quality of the housing collateral and other factors that might also shape trend outcomes in the county. We also explicitly control for other channels in various specifications. These other channels include the housing or collateral market; labor market; and bank lending channels. And because certain business types, like sole proprietorships, are more dependent on housing collateral for external finance, we can use specifications that exploit business-level heterogeneity to help parse further channels that emphasize house prices. We explain the second step first.

To measure a business’ potential exposure to the refinancing channel,Exposureij2009Q1⁠, we use proxies based on the size of the business, namely, the number of employees; past credit usage or liquidity; and the sector in which the business operates. Together, these proxies can help measure a business’ potential dependence on local cash flow and thus, its sensitivity to changes in local household disposable income.

Under the maintained hypothesis that QE1 refinancing shocks reduce the likelihood of business failure for smaller or illiquid businesses, we would expect the coefficients for the post-QE1 shocksj,1interacted with business size to be negative and statistically different from the pre-Q1 shocks,{sj,−3,sj,−2,sj,−1}⁠: Smaller businesses, especially those in the retail sector, are more dependent on local cash flow and often less able to access external sources of finance, making them less likely to exit when exposed to increased QE1 refi activity and more local spending. But if the shock variables measure latent factors within the county that do not also change upon the QE1 announcement week, then the coefficients for the pre- and post-shock variables should be statistically similar.

For example, if the shocks proxy for slowly evolving county-level factors like income and the aforementioned factors that affect business survival, thenγ1⁠, the coefficient for the post-QE shock, should be similar toγ−1⁠, the coefficient for the shock immediately preceding QE. Note that because we use relatively high-frequency weekly data, it is unlikely that aggregate news events, like other central bank announcements or broader economic conditions contaminate inference. Furthermore, to make progress on isolating the relative importance of the various mechanisms, some specifications allow business survival to depend on measures of housing and labor market dynamics; a county’s exposure to federal fiscal policy; and banks exposed to QE. This is done by simultaneously interacting these variables with a business’ potential sensitivity to local cash flows. We also conduct this survival analysis over the period 2009Q1–2009Q4, when QE1 was the only QE tool in place and over the longer 2009Q4–2015Q4 period. In these extended specifications, we include shocks related to QE2 and QE3 and conduct a battery of additional robustness tests.

To describe the construction of the refinancing shocks, letRjtdenote the number of refinances in countyjover periodt⁠. Building on this literature, letFjt(gjt,ejt)denote the fraction of households in countyjover periodtthat can profitably exercise the option to refinance their mortgages over periodtgiven the underlying rate gap and home equity distribution. This parameter proxies for county-level differences in income, education, age, financial literacy, or the other factors that can induce refi delays. Δ r j t = Δ f j t + Δ m j t . (3)

The shockssjtare shocks to the rate gap and or home equity in the county that change the value of the refi option and drive the change in refi activity. Because QE1 unexpectedly increased the rate gap, counties in which households had positive equity and paid attention to the announcement would have experienced a surge in QE1-induced refi activity in the weeks immediately after QE1. If in turn those households that refinanced spend some fraction of their debt service savings on local goods and services, then we would expect that nearby businesses most dependent on local spending to disproportionately benefit from QE1.

To reiterate the concern on mechanism, while the post-QE1 shocksj,1likely causally reflect the impact of QE1 on refi activity in the county, counties that respond more to the QE1 announcement might have had a left skewed home equity distribution before QE1 or lower unemployment. And the preexisting health of the county’s housing and labor market could independently affect business outcomes post-QE1. In what follows then, we consider specifications that not only include county fixed effects to absorb these latent factors but also allow the impact of business exposure to also vary with a variety of proxies for the housing and labor market condition in the county pre-QE1.

At the risk of understating the size of the refi channel, we also consider specifications that allow the impact of business exposure to depend on post-QE1 county-level indicators of housing and labor market conditions. The reduction in interest rates and overall uncertainty following QE1 could increase house prices (home equity) and employment relatively faster post-QE1 in those counties with large post-QE1 shockssj,1⁠. In this way, QE1 could relax business collateral constraints, affecting local business outcomes independent from the refi channel. We reiterate however that less of a concern is the case in which large post-QE1 refinancing shockssj,1causally improve local housing and labor market conditions—say through more renovation spending—and then through this pathway, also improve local business outcomes. Finally, we show that our basic results are not sensitive to the AR(1) assumption used to constructsj,1⁠. Next, we describe the data and validate these shocks.3

1.2 Data

1.2.1 Credit bureau data

Our information on businesses come from Experian’s BizAgg database.4This is a new opportunity to study the impact of monetary policy on businesses that are usually unrecorded in other databases. We observe the entire bureau data in 2009Q1, in 2009Q4, and again in 2015Q4. Using the bureau’s data to understand the transmission of refi shocks on to business outcomes comes with several advantages and disadvantages. Because the bureau obtains credit data from banks, trade credit providers, credit card companies and other sources of business credit, it includes most credit using businesses in the United States. This coverage allows us to understand the impact of monetary policy on sectors well beyond manufacturing and the related sectors that dominate databases comprising public companies. This is the main focus of most studies.

There are stark differences in sectoral composition across the two databases. “Miscellaneous retail” is the second-most common type of business in BizAgg, accounting for 9%of businesses; these businesses compose only 1%of Compustat business. Using the three snapshots in the data available to us, we can identify exit, entry, and credit usage patterns in response to refi shocks. Moreover, the information on credit usage, age, size, and the credit score assigned by the bureau are especially valuable in measuring financial constraints of smaller businesses. A standard concern in the financial constraint literature is that because of the precautionary motive, cash holdings might reflect anticipated financial constraints rather liquidity. But in the bureau data, we can identify when businesses have fully drawn down their credit lines and are likely facing large working capital shortfalls. The survival of these businesses would be expected to be most sensitive to any increase in local demand emanating from refinancing shocks. We supplement this measure of liquidity with business credit scores, and the age and number of their credit relationships (“aged” credit lines). Credit scores and the number of “aged” credit lines are used by lenders to evaluate credit risk and determine the stability of a business’s cash flow.

We acknowledge important shortcomings with the bureau’s data. Unlike the Census’s and other common data sets, the bureau records no precise information on business sales, income, assets and other balance sheet and income items. Thus, we cannot directly model the impact of exposure to the refi shock on these typical outcome variables. Also, unlike the case with consumer credit bureaus, not all lenders might report to the bureau a business’ credit usage, leading to a geographically unrepresentative set of firms.Table 2investigates this concern. The first column shows the percentage of BizAgg businesses by broad census geographies. The second column shows that the geographic distribution of BizAgg businesses is nearly identical to the geographic distribution of the U.S. population. The geographic distribution of BizAgg businesses and both the number and dollar amount of Community Reinvestment Act (CRA) loans made by banks to small businesses is also nearly identical (columns 3 and 4), suggesting that BizAgg is likely to be geographically representative.

1.2.2 Constructing and validating refinancing shocks

To derive the sequence of refinancing shocks around QE1, we estimate Equation (4) using mortgage application data from the Federal Reserve’s confidential Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) data set. The data are observed at the county-week level from 2007 through 2014. QE1 was announced on November 25, 2008 (the week of November 23, 2008), andFigure 2plots the county-level distribution of shocks derived from the weekly refi application growth rate estimation, observed both 2 weeks before and 2 weeks after the QE1 announcement week. The distribution of post-QE1 mortgage refi application shocks shifts rightward. Two weeks after QE1, the mean growth rate shock is 27.7%⁠, or about 10-percentage-points higher, than the mean shock 2 weeks before the policy. The post-QE1 shock distribution is also more spatially unequal. For example, the ratio of the 90th to 10th percentile shock distribution 2 weeks after QE1 is−2.3; the same ratio computed based on the distribution 2 weeks before the announcement is−1.5.

1.2.3 Information content of the shock

To gauge the information content of the refi shocks,Table 4shows that the refi applications shocks in the weeks immediately after the QE1 announcement significantly predict actual refinancing outcomes at the county-level during the overall QE1 implementation period. The dependent variable is the percentage of existing mortgages in a county that is subsequently refinanced over the period in which QE1 was active (November 23, 2008, to June 2010) based on the McDash data. The announcement week refi shock (November 23, 2008) as well as the refi applications shock 2 weeks after the QE1 announcement significantly predict the percentage of mortgages that is ultimately refinanced in the county during the entire QE1 implementation period. A one-standard-deviation increase in either shock variable is associated with a 0.3-percentage-point increase in the percentage of mortgages refinanced over QE1.

Moreover, further validating our approach is the fact that the shock variables in the 3 weeks immediately before the announcement are uncorrelated with subsequent refinancing activity. This fact suggests that the sequence of refi shocks do not contain persistent latent factors that drive refinancing activity. Instead, the refi application shocks immediately after the QE1 announcement capture state dependent responses to the drop in mortgage rates induced by the policy. Column 2 includes the logarithm of median income in the county, while column 3 adds population density and other demographic variables. The stability of the shock coefficients when including these demographic variables further suggests that the shock variables reflect the cross-county variation in the response to QE1 rather than persistent demographic factors that also drive the refinancing decision. To be sure, when added controls have limited variability, coefficient stability can be an imperfect proxy for omitted variables (Oster 2019).6

Table 5helps to interpret further the refi shock variables. The sample of households that respond immediately to the policy announcement and submit refinancing applications immediately after QE1 are likely more attentive households with positive equity who expect large welfare gains from refinancing: either by reducing debt service costs on their existing principal balance or possibly through accessing additional home equity at reduced interest rates (“cash-out” refinancing). The evidence inTable 5suggests that the refi shocks immediately after QE1 is associated with increased mortgage refinancing activity that on average reduced household debt service costs but did not increase cash out behavior, making it plausible that these shocks can affect business outcomes through increased local spending.

The first three columns ofTable 5focus on the cash out response. Several weeks can elapse before a mortgage refinancing application recorded in HMDA appears as an originated loan in the monthly McDash data. To accommodate this lag in the McDash data, we begin with January 2009. For each refinanced loan in January 2009 in a county, we compute the sum of the difference between the existing loan balance and the new loan amount originated under the refinancing; we then divide this difference by the number of mortgages refinanced in the county in January 2009 (column 1). If the refi shock variables capture increased equity extractions on account of QE1, then we would expect a negative coefficient: counties that responded more to the QE1 shock had smaller (and negative) mean differences between the retired principal and the new loan amount refinanced. However, if the shocks measure increased refinancing activity aimed at reducing debt service burdens, then the coefficient should be positive: new loan amounts are less than or equal to the retired debt: Households reduce debt service costs after QE1 without large equity extractions.

Table 5averages the refi shock in the 3 weeks after QE1 (inclusive of the QE1 announcement week). This is called the “post-QE1” shock; the “pre-QE1” shock is the average of the shocks 3 weeks before the announcement week. From column 1, moving from a county at the 10th to the 90th percentile of the average post-QE1 shock distribution is associated with about a $6,430 reduction in the mean principal “savings” in the county. The mean pre-QE1 shock variable is statistically insignificant. That is, in counties with increased refinancing activity in the weeks immediately after QE1, homeowners tended to extract less equity. To model the lags from application to origination, columns 2 and 3 use the mean principal savings in February and March of 2009, respectively. The coefficient for the post-QE1 shock remains significant but declines in the months after mid-December 2008; the pre-QE1 shock variable is never significant and is much smaller.

Table 5next examines the correlation between the QE1 refi shocks and the mean monthly payment “savings.” From the McDash data, for each mortgage that was refinanced in January 2009 in a county, we compute the sum of the difference between monthly payments before the loan is refinanced, and the new mortgage payment after the refinancing; as before, this difference is divided by the number of loans refinanced in the county in January 2009. Consistent with the previous principal “savings” results, the coefficient suggests that moving from the 10th to the 90th percentile in the cross-county distribution of the post-QE1 shock variable is associated with a $59.5 a month decrease in the mean monthly payment in the county, or a present value savings of about $4,400 over the typical 7-year life of a mortgage. By March 2009, a similar increase in the post-QE1 shock implies a mean monthly savings from refinancing mortgage debt of about $36.8.

Given that the QE1 refi shocks in this paper are associated with sizeable debt service reductions rather than cash out refinances, these shocks can affect business outcomes through increased local spending. Households tend to spend more of their monthly savings from refinancing mortgage debt on “regular living expenses,” that is, expenses that can directly affect local demand. The NY Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Expectations (SCE), for example, suggests that for monthly payment savings, 40%of borrowers use these savings to “pay for other expenses,” where “other” means other than paying down debt, large purchases or home renovation.7In contrast, about 40%of the savings in cash out refinances are used to pay off other nonmortgage debt, while 37%is used for home improvement. Only about 1.8%is spent on “regular living expenses.” Thus, the post-QE1 refi shocksj,1contains salient economic information for local business outcomes.

1.2.4 Correlates of the shock

Table 6examines the correlates of the QE1 shock variables. Counties that respond the most to the QE1 announcement might have higher income or better-educated households (i.e., households more attentive to monetary policy announcements) or ex ante might benefit from ex ante favorable labor and housing market conditions (i.e., lower unemployment, or a smaller fraction of households with negative equity). Some of these preexisting factors, like labor and housing market dynamics, could independently affect business outcomes post-QE1. These indicators are observed in November 2008.

article.2. Business-Level Results:
This section examines the impact of exposure to QE1-induced refinancing shocks on business level outcomes both in the short run, 2009Q1–2009Q4, and over the medium term, 2009Q4–2015Q4. We measure outcomes both at the extensive margin (whether a business survives) and at the intensive margin (the level of financial distress among those businesses that remain). We begin with the short run.

2.1 Short run, 2009Q1–2009Q4

2.1.1 Basic associations and alternative shocks

Figure 3shows the point estimates from the most general specification possible. The dependent variable in the regression underlyingFigure 3is the probability that a business that was present in the credit bureau in 2009Q1 exits the database by 2009Q4. Unlike distressed public firms, which file for bankruptcy to restructure or liquidate, most small private businesses simply cease operations when they become insolvent, making “exit” a useful extensive margin indicator of distress. The regression uses six HMDA weekly AR(1) refinancing shocks centered on the week of QE1; these shocks begin 3 weeks before the announcement week and end 2 weeks after. Because there can be serial correlation in the seriessjt⁠, we include pre-QE1 shocks to partial out the effects of recent pre-QE1 shocks, allowing us to more cleanly identify the impact of QE1 related refinancing shocks on firm survival.

Next, we interact these six shocks with five separate business-size indicator variables: fewer than 5 employees; 5–9 employees; 10–19 employees; 20–49 employees; and 50–99 employees. These are the most granular size measures in the database. This yields 30 coefficients. Note that the business size indicator variables are also linearly included in the regressions, while the HMDA refinancing shocks are linearly absorbed in the county fixed effects.Figure 3shows the coefficients (dots) and 95%confidence banks (lines); note that the six HMDA refinancing shocks are standardized to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one to facilitate comparison.

Across all business sizes, there is no “pre-trend.” None of the shocks in the 3 weeks before the QE1 announcement week affects business survival, regardless of business size. This in part reflects the fact that refi activity in the weeks before QE1 was much smaller than refi activity after QE1.8Instead, businesses with fewer than five employees are about 1-percentage-point more likely to survive through the end of 2009 in a county one-standard-deviation away from the mean shock in the week of QE1 relative to the base category of businesses over 100 employees (panel A). Moreover, the effects of exposure to the refinancing shock on survival declines somewhat as business size increases. For businesses with 20–49 employees, exposure to a one-standard-deviation increase in the QE1 week shock increases the survival likelihood by about 0.75 percentage points (panel A). At the 50–99 employees size bin, the effect is not statistically significant. The absence of “pre-trends” inFigure 3using weekly data suggests that refi-activity related to QE1 likely shaped business survival in the short run.

Next, we use a more parsimonious version of the regression shown inFigure 3to gauge the robustness of this evidence to alternative shock measures. This regression uses two indicator variables based on size. The first equals one if a business has fewer than 20 employees, and zero otherwise (“small”); the second indicator variable equals one if the business has between 20 and 50 employees, and zero otherwise (“medium”); and the omitted category are “big” businesses, that is, those with more than 50 employees. To reduce the parameter space further, the regression also averages the weekly shocks in the 3 weeks before (“pre”) and in the 3 weeks after the QE1 announcement (“post”) November 23, 2008; note that “post” includes the announcement week shock in the average. This regression thus estimates four interaction terms.

Column 1 ofTable B.1inInternet Appendixuses the baseline AR(1) HMDA refinancing application shocks, while columns 2 through 4 replace the AR(1) shock with shocks from AR(2), AR(3) and AR(4) refinancing application processes, respectively. Column 5 uses shocks estimated from an AR(1) process based on the monthly growth in originated refinancing in the county from McDash. Monthly data are coarser, and we use December 2008 as the post-QE1 shock, and November 2008 as the pre-shock.Figure 4compares the marginal impact of the five different shocks estimated in the five separate models. Each panel ofFigure 4shows the marginal impact of moving from a county at the 10th to the 90th percentile of a particular shock across the five models on business survival for the “medium” and for “small” businesses. The impact of these various refi shocks on business survival is virtually identical across the five models.

For example, panel A ofFigure 4shows that moving from a county at the 10th percentile of the post-QE1 HMDA AR(1) baseline shock to one at the 90th percentile is associated with a 1.4-percentage-point decline in the probability that a medium-sized business exits the sample between 2009Q1 and 2009Q4. The unconditional probability of exit for these businesses is 35.7%⁠. The regression that uses the AR(2) shock implies a marginal effect of 1.7 percentage points, while the AR(3), AR(4), and McDash regressions imply a 1.5-, 1.4-, and 1.4-percentage-point decline in the probability of exit for medium-sized businesses (“20–50 employees”). In no case is there a “pre-trend” (panels C and D). In the case of small businesses (“fewer than 20 employees”), some evidence indicates that higher-order post-QE1 HDMA AR processes imply even bigger effects on business survival likelihoods (panel B). This evidence shows that the impact of refi-activity immediately after QE1 on business survival in the short run is not sensitive to the AR process used to estimate the shock, and we continue to use the AR(1) HMDA shock as our baseline measure of QE1.

2.1.2 Channels

Table 7considers the various channels through which these QE1 shocks might have affected business survival in the short run. To do so concisely, we use the most parsimonious specification. This is an indicator variable that equals one if a county is in the top tercile of the post-QE1 refi shock, and zero otherwise; next, we interact this variable with the two business size indicator variables. There are now only two relevant interaction terms to measure exposure to the post-QE1 shock; we drop the pre-QE1 shock variable, as it is never statistically or economically significant (Figures 3and4). The baseline also excludes the middle tercile counties from the sample, thereby comparing exposure to the refinancing shock in the top tercile counties to those in the bottom of the post-QE1 refi distribution.9

From column 1 ofTable 7, a medium-sized business in a top-tercile-post-QE1-shock county is about 1.3-percentage-points less likely to exit relative to a similar sized business located in a bottom-tercile county (⁠p-value<.01⁠). The effect on very small businesses is about half as big, but not precisely estimated. Column 2 ofTable 7interacts the business size indicator variables with county median income; the latter variable is itself linearly absorbed in the county fixed effect. Although income was a robust determinant of the post-QE1 shock (column 7 ofTable 7), column 2 ofTable 7shows that the point estimate on the post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients is little changed. But to be conservative, we include the business size indicator variables interacted with median income as a baseline control.

Columns 3 and 4 assess the labor market dynamics channel. Column 3 interacts the two size indicator variables with the unemployment rate in the county in November 2008. A one-standard-deviation decrease in the unemployment rate is associated with a 0.42-percentage-point reduction in the probability that a medium-sized business exits between 2009Q1 and 2009Q4 (⁠p-value=.12⁠). The point estimate on the post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients are unchanged. Column 4 probes further the labor market channel, replacing the unemployment rate in November 2008 with the change in construction unemployment over the previous 6 months (May-November 2008). This variable is closely linked to the ex ante health of the local housing and labor markets and nontradeable demand. The point estimate on the post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients are unchanged.Table B.2inInternet Appendixconsiders robustness exercises using the change in construction employment over the previous 3 months, as well as over the previous year. The point estimate on the post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients are unchanged.

Columns 5–8 ofTable 7use more direct measures of the local housing market. Column 5 interacts the size measures with the fraction of homeowners with negative equity, while column 6 uses the median home equity in the county at the time of QE1. The point estimates on the post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients are unchanged. Column 7 considers changes in local house prices. It interacts the business-size indicator variables with the change in house prices the previous six months (May to November 2008). It also interacts these size variables with the change in house prices in the county 6 months after the QE1 announcement (November 2008-May 2009). The former controls for house price dynamics in the county the previous 6 months, while the latter suite of interaction terms directly controls for the fact that QE1 could also increase local house prices in areas with large refi shocks, altering business collateral values and possibly house price expectations. Consistent with the housing channel, small and medium-sized businesses are less likely to exit in counties with faster house price growth in the 6 months after QE1. However, the point estimates on the post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients are virtually unchanged.Table B.2inInternet Appendixconsiders house price dynamics around the QE1 announcement at 3- and 12-month frequencies as well. The post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients are virtually unchanged.

Realized changes in house prices after QE1 might not fully proxy for the house price channel. Expectations of future house price appreciation could be positively correlated with QE1 refinancing activity, and these expectations could also independently affect business survival. To further gauge the salience of this channel then, column 8 uses the share of land that is unavailable for building (Saiz 2010). House price growth is more sensitive to shocks in areas with inelastic land supply, where geography or zoning restricts the land area available for building (Glaeser et al. 2008). Thus, according toMian and Sufi (2009), expectations of house price growth should be weaker in areas with a larger share of land available for building, and stronger in areas where land supply is inelastic. This logic then implies that interacting business size with the share of land unavailable for building in the county can absorb the house price growth expectations-based channel. From column 8, the point estimates on the post-QE1 refi shock interaction coefficients are again virtually unchanged.

We have seen evidence that controlling for local labor and housing market dynamics both before and after QE1 do not alter the impact of the refi shocks on business exit rates. This suggests that the refi shocks likely affected business outcomes through reduced debt service burdens that resulted in higher local spending, and not via the labor and housing market channels. To provide more direct evidence of this mechanism, column 9 interacts the business size indicator variables with the mean principal savings in the county in January 2009. Recall that this variable is the sum of the difference between the existing loan balance and the new refinanced loan amounts; this difference is divided by the number of mortgages refinanced in the county in January 2009. Thus, higher values correspond to less “cash out” refinances, bigger debt service reductions and more potential local demand.

The point estimates on these interaction terms are negative and significant. For example, a one-standard-deviation increase in the mean principal savings implies a 1-percentage-point decrease in the small business exit rate (fewer than 20 employees). Moreover, for the first time the refi shock coefficients shrink, by about 40%in this case, and are no longer statistically significant. All this suggests that reduced debt service burdens and higher local spending might be the salient channel through which the refi shocks affected exit rates. In this case, we should expect the impact of the refi shocks to be largest among those businesses most sensitive to increased local spending. Next, we consider this hypothesis.

2.1.3 Business heterogeneity

Using the baseline specification, column 1 ofTable 8restricts the sample to retail establishments, that is, SIC codes between 52 and 59. These are food stores; drinking and eating establishments; and apparel and other businesses that are normally highly dependent on local demand for their cash flow. The implied effect of the refi shock on the exit probability is over 4 times bigger than in the full sample. For a retail business with fewer than 20 employees (“small”) in a county in top tercile of the QE1 shock distribution, the probability of exit declines by 4.5 percentage points. For a “medium-sized” (20–50 employees) business, top tercile exposure implies a 5-percentage-point drop in the exit rate. Note that the unconditional exit rate is 28.7%for this subsample. Column 2 uses the subset of “food” businesses, namely, the canonical nontradeable business. The point estimates are about 15%–20%larger than overall retail. Conversely, if these results reflect local demand, then the impact of post-QE1 refi shocks on manufacturing business survival should be much smaller, as this sector is less dependent on local demand for cash flow. On the other hand, if the collateral channel is salient, then there should be only minor differences in survival among similarly sized businesses regardless of their primary sector. From column 3 ofTable 8, the post-QE1 refi shock has no significant impact on business survival in the manufacturing sector.

The remaining columns ofTable 8examine whether financial factors amplify the effects of the refinancing channel on business survival. If the QE1 shock increased local spending, then the shock should have the largest impact on business survival among the most illiquid or financially constrained businesses. Because these businesses have the greatest shortfall between internally generated cash flow and required working capital to continue as a going concern, their survival would be most sensitive to any increase in local cash flow and asset prices.

To measure illiquidity, column 4 ofTable 8restricts the sample to the set of businesses that reached their maximum borrowing capacity in 2009Q1 on at least one of the credit lines reported to the bureau. Column 5 uses the sample of liquid businesses: those that did not exhaust a credit line in 2009Q1. The impact of the refi shocks on survival is markedly different across the two subsamples. For illiquid businesses with fewer than 20 employees (column 4), top-tercile exposure to the refi shock reduces the likelihood of exit by about 5 percentage points; for more liquid businesses (column 5), the point estimate is 80%smaller and not significant (⁠p-value=.15⁠). Similarly, for medium-sized illiquid businesses, top-tercile exposure has an effect over three times bigger than equally sized more liquid businesses.

Columns 6 and 7 uses Experian’s credit score rating in 2009 Q1 to classify business default risk. Experian estimates that businesses classified as “High” credit risk have about a 50%chance of becoming delinquent on a credit account—these are the most credit rationed businesses and most dependent on local cash flow—the sample used in column 6. Businesses classified as “Low” credit risk, the sample in column 7, have a 1.7%chance of becoming delinquent on a credit account. The evidence suggests that the survival of “High” credit risk businesses are about 2-3 times more sensitive to the refinancing shocks than the “Low” risk subsample; among this latter subsample, the point estimates are also not significant.

2.1.4 Financial distress at the intensive margin

This subsection examines the impact of the QE1 refinancing shock on financial distress at the intensive margin. Among the firms that remained in the database through 2009Q4, greater household spending driven by the QE1 refinancing shock can reduce the incidence of financial distress. We first measure financial distress using credit usage data. Because businesses that face working capital shortfalls are more likely to exhaust their borrowing capacity from at least one credit line, be it bank, credit card or trade credit, the dependent variable in columns 1–3 equals one if a business borrowed up to its credit limit in at least one of its credit lines in 2009Q4, and zero if it has not exhausted its borrowing capacity on a credit line.

Using the same top tercile-bottom tercile design previously, column 1 ofTable 9shows within the full sample that small businesses in the top tercile of the post-QE1 refi shock distribution are about 1.2-percentage-points less likely to exhaust their borrowing capacity. For medium-sized businesses, the impact is about half. Retail businesses, and food businesses in particular are highly dependent on local demand; columns 2 and 3 restrict the sample to these sectors, respectively. In the case of retail, the implied effect of exposure to QE1 in reducing the likelihood of distress is about 20%larger, while in the case of food establishments, this effect is nearly double that observed in column 1. That is, exposure to QE1 refinancing not only reduces business exits but also financial distress.

The remaining columns ofTable 9expand on this theme. The credit bureau uses a proprietary five-point credit rating for each business in order to measure business default risk.10The algorithm behind this rating uses data on a business’s existing debt capacity, past repayment behavior, and data on consumer behavior in the local area. This rating is commonly used by lenders to help make lending decisions, and it provides a relatively comprehensive indicator of financial distress: a business that experiences a downgrade is more likely to be in financial distress, and face both an increase in borrowing costs and a contraction in credit availability.

Using the ratings data both in 2009Q4 and in 2009Q1, we create an indicator variable that equals one if a business experienced a downgrade in 2009Q4 relative to its rating in 2009Q1. The point estimates on the exposure-size interaction terms in columns 4 and 5 are negative, but not significant at conventional levels. In the case of food establishments, however, there is significant evidence that exposure to the top tercile post-QE1 shock significantly reduced the likelihood of a downgrade. For small and medium-sized food businesses, the implied effect is a 3.5- and 3-percentage-point reduction, respectively. In both cases, the unconditional probability of a downgrade is about 47%⁠. Thus, exposure to the QE1 refinancing shock both increased the probability of business survival in 2009 and reduced the likelihood of financial distress among surviving businesses through the end of 2009.
article.3. Persistence, 2009Q4–2015Q4:
Can QE1 and the refinancing channel shape business outcomes in the long run? Households that refinanced during QE1 benefitted from permanently lower debt service costs and higher disposable income.11Thus, areas more exposed to the QE1 shock might have persistently higher spending on local goods and services relative to less exposed areas. These persistent spatial differences in local spending can then lead to significant differences in the probability of business survival in the medium term. Persistent spatial differences in local spending can also induce variation in the local economic structure. For example, businesses or national chains can become entrenched in areas that initially lost small businesses, making it difficult for smaller businesses to enter these local markets in the future.

3.1 Basic results

Table 10investigates this persistence hypothesis. Using the credit bureau data in 2015Q4, we create an indicator variable that equals one if a business that was present in 2009Q4 exited the database by 2015Q4, and zero if the business remained in the database. We continue to use the top tercile-bottom tercile baseline specification.12Column 1 uses the full sample of businesses. Over this 6-year period, small businesses, those with fewer than 20 employees, located in top tercile counties are 2.6-percentage-points less likely to exit; for medium-sized businesses, the relative decline in the exit probability is 1.7 percentage points. In the case of the former, the unconditional probability of exit is 47.8%⁠, and 33.9%in the case of the latter.

In the retail subsample, the relative likelihood of exit declines by about 3.8 percentage points for a business with fewer than 20 employees that is located in the top tercile of the post-QE1 refi distribution. As we saw in the 2009 analysis, if increasing local demand was the key channel through which exposure to the refi shock helped businesses survive, then we would expect the impact on survival to be relatively large among the sample of illiquid businesses in 2009Q4. The results in column 4, which restrict the sample to businesses facing working capital shortfalls in 2009Q4, comport with this expectation. Columns 5 and 6 extend this approach, restricting the sample to illiquid retail (column 5) and illiquid food (column 6) businesses. The impact of the post-QE1 shock on survival rises sharply for these subsamples. Even in the small subsample of illiquid food businesses, the probability of exit conditional on top tercile exposure to the post-QE1 shock drops by 8 percentage points for small businesses, and 6.9 percentage points for medium-sized businesses.

To understand better how QE1-induced local demand shocks might interact with business illiquidity and access to external sources of finance to shape long-run survivalTable 11exploits information about business age. Studies using public firms show that age and size proxy for financing constraints (Hadlock and Pierce 2010). In our sample of very small private businesses, younger and very small businesses likely have less access to external finance, and conversely, are more dependent on internal sources of revenue in order to survive. Therefore, if QE1 persistently raised local demand and the cash flow of local businesses, then this logic predicts that the post-QE1 shock should have larger effects on business survival among the younger firms in 2009Q4, especially those that are particularly dependent on local demand, such as retail and food businesses.

3.1.1 Other Channels: Bank lending, monetary, and fiscal policy

This subsection evaluates the importance of other channels through which the QE1 refinancing shock affected business survival. In particular, Luck and Zimmermann (2018),Chakraborty et al. (2020),Darmouni and Rodnyansky (2017)andFoley-Fisher et al. (2016)all observe that various QE policies can expand the supply of credit available to businesses. This credit supply channel implies that because the net worth of local banks that have large holdings of mortgage-backed securities increase after QE1, these banks will expand lending to local businesses. The resultant credit supply expansion could increase the long-term survival of local businesses. In the current setting, if banks that are most exposed to QE1 through this net worth channel are also located in the same counties with the largest post-QE1 refi shocks, then the previous results might reflect the effects of QE1 operating through the credit supply-bank balance sheet channel rather than through local demand. Let us then clarify better the pathways through which the refi shock might influence business outcomes in the long run.

To this end, columns 1 and 2 ofTable 12use the identical county-level bank exposure data from Luck and Zimmermann (2018).13These bank exposure measures weight each bank’s ratio of mortgage backed securities (MBS) to total assets by the bank’s market share inside the county. In column 1, this county-level weighted average of MBS/Assets is based on each bank’s deposit market share in the county. Column 2 uses the bank’s Small Business Lending (SBL) market share as the relevant weighting factor when computing the county-level weighted exposure to MBS/Assets in the banking system. Note that the SBL weighted exposure measure should be especially apt given that most of the businesses in the sample are small and medium sized. As in Luck and Zimmermann (2018), we include these weighted averages computed for QE1, QE2, and QE3.

Two facts are apparent from this exercise. First, the point estimates on the post-QE1 shocks do not change from our benchmark specification: the impact of the refi shock on business survival does not appear to be driven by this credit supply channel. The second fact is that these county-level bank exposure measures do not give consistent results in this setting. In column 1, QE3 exposure does seem to reduce the likelihood of exit for small business, but this result reverses when exposure is measured using SBL derived weights (column 2). This ambiguity possibly reflects the substitution documented elsewhere in the literature (Chakraborty et al. 2020), as QE exposed banks could shift lending toward the mortgage market, and away from business lending.14

The longer 6-year window spans other major policy interventions. And another concern centers on whether the long-run results reflect QE1 or QE2 (November 2010) or QE3 (September 2012). The 2009Q1–2009Q4 analysis is not susceptible to this concern, and the similarity of these estimates across the two-time horizons point to QE1 as the principal factor. Nevertheless, column 3 constructs identical HMDA refinancing application shocks in the 3-week window after QE2 and QE3, and includes business size interaction terms using these shocks. The point estimates on the post-QE1 shock measures are unchanged. Finally, column 4 considers the fiscal policy channel.

The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) was passed in early 2009, leading to around $800 billion in federal government spending over the next 8 years. While much of this spending was on large infrastructure programs, it did include direct cash transfers to households and funded unemployment insurance extensions. If these transfers are positively correlated with the post-QE1 refi shock, then the previous estimates could be biased. To check whether ARRA spending affects these results, column 4 interacts business size with data from ProPublica on per capita county-level ARRA spending through 2012.15The results are unchanged.

3.1.2 Housing (redux)

This subsection examines whether the long-run effects of the refi shock on business outcomes operate through the housing market. To check the relevance of this channel, column 1 ofTable 13includes the average change in house prices over the period 2010-2015 in the county interacted with the business size indicator variables. As before, the average itself is linearly absorbed in the county fixed effects. The point estimates on the post-QE1 shock variables are unchanged. The remaining columns ofTable 13use information about the ownership structure of the business to measure better the salience of the housing channel. The logic of these tests builds on the fact that sole proprietorships generally obtain working capital and investment finance directly through the owner’s balance sheet. For sole proprietorship then, the owner’s housing equity will be a prime source of finance for these businesses. Thus, if the refi shocks operate through the housing channel, then the survival of sole proprietorships might be particularly sensitive to changes in house prices.

3.2 QE1 and the pattern of local activity

3.2.1 Spatial variation in retail

This subsection examines whether QE1-induced refi activity affected the pattern of local economic activity. To wit, differences in the survival of retail businesses on account of the variation in QE1 refinancing shocks can potentially affect the spatial variation in retail intensity. The dependent variable in column 1 ofTable 14is thus the change in the retail location quotient in a county between 2009Q4 and 2015Q4.16The change in the location quotient measures the change in the relative intensity retail business in the county. The OLS point estimate in column 1 suggests that increased refinancing activity during the entire QE1 implementation period—the percentage of mortgages refinanced in the county from December 2008 through June 2010—is positively associated with an increase in retail intensity, but this variable is not significant at conventional levels (⁠p-value=.21⁠). Column 2 instruments overall QE1 refinancing activity with the top tercile post-QE1 shock dummy. The IV-point estimate is positive, significant and economically large. It suggests that a one-standard-deviation increase in overall refi activity is associated with a 15-percentage-point or 0.52-standard-deviation increase in retail intensity. Column 3 reports the reduced-form correlation between the change in the location quotient and the top tercile post-QE1 shock dummy.

Taken together, this evidence suggests that while incumbent retail businesses were more likely to exit in counties less exposed to QE1, the number of new entrants in these areas did not fully replace the exits, leading to an overall net decline in retail intensity in counties less exposed to the QE1 refi shock.

3.2.2 Chain store spending

If indeed small retail business intensity declined in areas less exposed to the QE1 refi shock, then we would expect a relative shift in consumer retail spending in these areas toward large national chains, such as CVS, Kroger, and other similar chain stores. Such a shift in spending toward large national chain stores could also help explain the persistent decline in small retail intensity in counties less exposed to the QE1 refi shock. As local consumers shift their retail spending toward large national retail chains in these counties, small retail businesses might find it less attractive to enter these local markets, as they would face the threat of limit pricing or the higher fixed costs of advertising needed to compete for market share against a large well-financed incumbent. To corroborate the main results then, this subsection uses scanner-level data from major national chain stores (the IRI data set) to study whether exposure to QE1 is associated with such shifts in consumer spending patterns.17The IRI data set includes scanner-level weekly spending data on about 75 food and beverage items. The data are available only for a subset of large national chains, and we aggregate the data into a county-month panel over the period 2008-2011.

The dependent variable inTable 15is the logarithmic value of all consumer retail (food) spending summed across all chain stores covered in the data set for a given county-month. The key variable of interest is the interaction between an indicator variable that equals one if a county is in the bottom tercile of the QE1 refi shock and an indicator variable that equals one in the post-QE1 period. The estimated coefficient for this variable measures whether chain-store retail spending in those counties least exposed to QE1-induced refinancing changed after QE1 relative to other counties and time periods. All regressions include county fixed effects as well as year-by-month fixed effects, and standard errors are clustered at the state level.

Taken together, these results imply that the relatively lower local demand in areas less responsive to QE1 refinancings, and the substitution in consumer spending toward large national chains can help explain the persistent decline in small retail intensity in counties less exposed to the QE1 refi shock.
article.4. Conclusion:
Using new data on very small and medium-sized mostly private businesses, this paper has studied the impact of the refinancing channel of monetary policy on business survival, liquidity and the long-run pattern of local economic activity in the short and medium runs. We show that QE1-induced refinancing activity had large effects on business outcomes, especially businesses operating in sectors that are dependent on local demand for revenue. Financial factors amplified the effects of the refinancing channel. Illiquid retail and food businesses were far more likely to survive during the early stages of the program’s implementation, that is, through the end of 2009, as well as some 6 years later. Similarly, the survival and stability of credit relationships among young and small businesses were particularly sensitive to QE1 refinancing activity. These longer-run effects shaped the pattern of county economic activity, as counties with less QE1 refinancing activity experienced a relative decline in retail and small businesses over time. We view the point estimates in this exercise as useful inputs for quantitative models that calibrate the heterogeneous consequences of central bank interventions on households, counties and businesses.

Together, these results suggest that by permanently altering local household debt service burdens and spending patterns, the refinancing channel of monetary policy can induce large spatial differences in economic activity. Moreover, the fact that the refinancing channel appears strongest after QE1 is consistent with theories that predict that the real effects of monetary policy might be state-dependent. That is, as most households refinance into lower cost debt, additional quantitative easing programs might have smaller real effects. It is also possible that the relatively large QE1 results reflect the effects of forward guidance. Because initiating QE extends the expected date of the next policy tightening cycle and signals the central bank’s commitment to prolonged policy easing, this forward guidance type argument notes that households’ marginal propensity to spend out of their QE refi savings might be bigger than otherwise, leading to greater cash flow at local businesses compared to a non-QE related refi activity (McKay et al. 2016;Bernanke 2022;Bhattarai et al. 2022). Future research with data on household consumption and local spending can make progress on these channels.
